titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Dick Gallup Papers
titlestmt.author: Michael L. Forstrom

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Friday June 13, 2025 at 23:06

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Box 30 (videocassette): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 31 (optical disk): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 34 (audiocassettes): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Granary Books, Inc. on the Ezra Pound Archive Fund, 2022.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into five series: I. Correspondence, 1959-2008. II. Writings, 1950-2019. III. Diaries and Notebooks, 1961-2003. IV. Teaching and Administrative Files, 1969-1981. V. Personal Papers, circa 1943-2016.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Richard John Gallup was born on July 3, 1941, in Greenfield, Massachusetts, to Harry Kenneth ("John") Gallup and Edna (White) Gallup. In late 1949 the Gallup family moved to Tulsa, Oklahoma, into a house across the street from Ron Padgett. The two boys became friends, and in 1958, while in high school, they founded, a small literary magazine, The White dove review , with another classmate, artist Joe Brainard. The review published Jack Kerouac, Allen Ginsberg, Leroi Jones (Amiri Baraka), and Robert Creeley, as well as a new friend in Tulsa, Ted Berrigan.

Gallup attended Tulane University for two years, then transferred to Columbia's School of General Studies, where he studied under the poet Kenneth Koch and completed a B.A. degree. During the 1960s and 1970s Gallup established himself as an important figure in the New York School of poets, publishing collections of his poetry and a play, including Hinges (Ted Berrigan's C Press, 1965), The Bingo (Peter Schjeldahl's Mother Press, 1966), Where I hang my hat (Harper & Row, 1970), and Above the tree line (Bill Berkson's Big Sky Books, 1976). He was a mainstay in the St. Mark's Poetry Project community, where he taught a workshop and gave numerous readings. He also taught poetry writing to children, sponsored by the Teachers & Writers Collaborative in New York City and by state arts councils in New Jersey, Connecticut, and New York. Along the way he received grants from the National Endowment for the Arts and the Fund for Poetry.

In 1973 Gallup and his family moved to West Virginia, where Gallup had been appointed the state poet-in-the-schools, and then to South Carolina, where he did similar work for a few years. Following these appointments, the family moved to northern California, where Gallup befriended poet and NPR commentator Andrei Codrescu. Separating from his wife, Gallup moved to Boulder to teach at Naropa University.

In 1981 Gallup settled in San Francisco, where for the rest of his life he led a somewhat secluded lifestyle, driving a taxi at night, reading science fiction, playing guitar, and raising his son, with whom he later shared an apartment. Although Gallup brought out one more collection, Plumbing the depths of folly (Smithereens Press, 1983), he stopped writing and promoting his work, though he did accept invitations to give readings in New York (2001) and Tulsa (2009), and he said yes when Alan Kornblum, a former student, proposed to bring out his Shiny pencils at the edge of things: New and selected poems (Coffee House Press, 2001).
Biographical sketch revised from Padgett, Ron. "Tribute." Poetry Society of America, https://poetrysociety.org/poems-essays/tributes/dick-gallup-1941-2021. Accessed 4 March 2025.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Dick Gallup Papers. Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Prior to the library's acquisition of Gallup's papers in 2022, Ron Padgett examined the papers and contributed to its arrangement.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Dick Gallup papers consist of correspondence, writings, diaries and notebooks, teaching and administrative files, and personal papers that document Gallup's personal and professional activities. The bulk of the papers range in date from Gallup's youth in the late 1950s to the 1980s. Correspondents include Bill Berkson, Ted Berrigan, Joe Brainard, Michael Brownstein, Tom Clark, Andrei Codrescu, Jack Collom, Alfred Corn, Theodore Enslin, Clayton Eshleman, Larry Fagin, Allen Ginsberg, Bobby Louise Hawkins, Anselm Hollo, Joanne Kyger, Ron Padgett, Ed Sanders, Tom Veitch, Anne Waldman, and Lewis Warsh.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Dick Gallup papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

corpname.part: St. Mark's Church In-the-Bowery (New York, N.Y.). Poetry Project

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Box 34 (audiocassettes): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I. contains Gallup's incoming correspondence from family, friends, writers and editors, publishers, educational institutions, and literary organizations. Correspondence spans from 1959 to 2008, though the bulk of the correspondence dates from the 1960s to 1980s, when Gallup was most active as a writer and most active within the poetry community in the United States. Correspondents include Gallup's early literary collaborators, Ron Padgett, Joe Brainard, and Ted Berrigan, and, after first moving to New York City in the 1960s, a widening network of other writers based chiefly in New York, California, and Colorado. Other correspondents include: Bill Berkson, Michael Brownstein, Tom Clark, Andrei Codrescu, Jack Collom, Alfred Corn, Theodore Enslin, Clayton Eshleman, Larry Fagin, Allen Ginsberg, Bobby Louise Hawkins, Anselm Hollo, Joanne Kyger, Ed Sanders, Tom Veitch, Anne Waldman, and Lewis Warsh.

In general, incoming letters address personal, professional, and literary matters. The largest file, for Ron Padgett, contains circa 92 pieces of correspondence. Padgett's correspondence to Gallup spans over four decades, dating from the summer of 1959, after Gallup graduated from Central High School, in Tulsa, Oklahoma, to 2003. Padgett's letters, many of which are multiple pages in length, discuss their poetry, work, travel, financial matters, family, and other writers and mutual friends. Other large files of circa 32 and 72 pieces of correspondence respectively can be found for Gallup's wife Carol (Clifford) Gallup and Marian Michaels (also Marian Fleischer or Crystal).

In addition to incoming letters and postcards to Gallup, there are carbons of outgoing letters to others scattered throughout the files. Enclosures accompanying incoming letters, such as drafts of writings and photographs, are documented in folder notes.
Small files of correspondence can also be found in Series IV. Teaching and Administrative Files and Series V. Personal Papers.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes correspondence relating to Naropa Institute business and community members. Individual correspondents include: Charles Bukowski, Robert Creeley, Fielding Dawson, Carl Dennis, Larry Fagin, Allen Ginsberg, Carl Rakosi, Anne Waldman, and Philip Whalen.

did.unittitle: University of California, San Diego. University Library

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two subseries: Writings of Dick Gallup and Writings of Others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series II. Writings includes drafts of work by Gallup and others. Gallup's writings are arranged by title and/or folder heading, with drafts and other material relating to published work, collections of poems for what appear to be unpublished manuscripts, and individual poems, essays, short prose pieces, and "school work" dating to the late 1950s.

There are drafts and other materials relating to Gallup's published works, including his play, The Bingo (New York: Mother Press, 1966), and the poetry collections, Hinges (New York: "C" Press, 1965), Where I hang my hat (New York: Harper & Row, 1970), Above the tree line (Bolinas: Big Sky Books, 1976), Plumbing the depths of folly (Boulder: Smithereens Press, 1983), and Shiny Pencils at the edge of things: New and selected poems (Minneapolis: Coffee House Press, 2000).

In addition to the materials relating to Gallup's published titles and what appear to be unpublished manuscripts of poems, there are numerous groupings of poems organized loosely by date. Gallup also preserved a folder of writings done in collaboration with other poets. His collaborators include Ted Berrigan, Michael Brownstein, Andrei Codrescu, Larry Fagin, Ron Padgett, Peter Schjeldahl, and Tom Veitch. There is also a prose manuscript, "The Planetary route," written in collaboration with Tom Veitch.

The Writings of Others subseries features runs of work by early collaborator Ted Berrigan and childhood friend and collaborator Ron Padgett. Works by or associated with Berrigan include typescript drafts of poems, some dedicated to Gallup, and numerous collaborations. Works include several small, handmade booklets or "bokes" as they were playfully named. Collaborators on these booklets include Joe Brainard and Lorenz Gude. Most of the work associated with Berrigan dates from the early to mid 1960s, when these poets resided in New York City.

The run of work by Ron Padgett consists of a diverse group of writings, some inscribed to Gallup or accompanied by letters, and spans a longer period of time, dating from the late 1950s to 2019. This group of work, including bokes and drafts of poems, autobiographical essays, college papers, and translations, documents the two poets lifelong friendship.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two subseries: Diaries and Calendars and Noteboooks.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p:
Series III. is organized by format for commercially printed volumes and various kinds of notebooks.
The Diaries and Calendars subseries consists of a run of printed red volumes, annual "National diaries" and "Daily reminders" published by the National Blank Book Company and Wilson Jones Company respectively. Gallup wrote lengthy entries in early years, from 1965 through 1971, and then used the volumes chiefly for shorter entries from 1971 onward for appointments, events, and more detailed daily schedules. Both publications include preliminary sections for addresses and ledgers following at the end of the volume. Gallup used these ledgers in early volumes to document his literary activity and income, specifically, publications, readings, and workshops. For example, he listed poems published, the publications in which he placed poems, and income received (when paid).

The Notebooks subseries consists of various kinds of hard and soft cover notebooks. The content of the notebooks is mixed and cuts across series for Writings, Diaries and Notebooks, and Teaching and Administrative Files.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes photographs and printed materials

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two subseries: Professional Files and Student Writing.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Gallup worked as a poet in the schools from the late 1960s to the mid 1970s before taking on teaching and administrative roles at the Naropa Institute in Boulder, Colorado in the late 1970s. While in Boulder, he also served as a poet in an Artist in School and Community program sponsored by the Colorado Council on the Arts & Humanities and on the advisory board of the Community Agencies Art Project (C.A.A.P.) sponosored by Naropa. Series IV. brings together Gallup's professional files relating to these various positions as well as poems written by primary through high school age students. Materials relate specifically to teaching jobs in New York City public schools (1969-circa 1972) and to later posts in West Virginia (1974), South Carolina (1975-1976), and Colorado (1977-1981).
Most correspondence relating to these professional positions is filed in Series I. Correspondence.

did.unittitle: Colorado Council on the Arts & Humanities
In-School Coordinator/Principal Report on Artist's Visit
"Book of methods for getting children interested in writing: A Handbook for teachers"

"Somebody handed me a pair of wings"
"A Special whistle: Charlotte-Mecklenburg Poets in the Schools Program, 1975-1976"
"There's room in everyone's heart for a rainbow"

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Box 30 (videocassette): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 31 (optical disk): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into four subseries: Financial and Legal Records, Photographs, Printed Material, and Other Papers.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series V. consolidates assorted other small groups of material within Gallup's papers, including financial and legal records, photographs, and printed materials. Materials range in date from 1943 to 2016, but the bulk of these materials date from the 1960s to early 1980s, the period in which Gallup appears to have been most active as a writer and most engaged with other poets within the U.S. literary community.
There are photographs of Gallup, family, friends, and fellow poets. Poets include Ted Berrigan, Michael Brownstein, Tom Clark, Allen Ginsberg, Ron Padgett, and Anne Waldman.

Within the Printed materials subseries, the event-related materials contain printed and mimeograph announcements for exhibitions, poetry readings, and other events. There are circa 66 announcements for readings by Gallup and other poets in the New York City area dating chiefly from the 1960s and 1970s. The announcements document readings by poets associated with the second wave of the New York School, including Gallup, Ron Padgett, Ted Berrigan, Bill Berkson, Joe Brainard, Anne Waldman, Lewis Warsh, Michael Brownstein, Peter Schjeldahl, David Antin, Frank Lima, and Bill Zavatsky. Other poets noted in these flyers include Kenward Elmslie, Larry Fagin, Kenneth Koch, Tony Towle, Gerard Malanga, Leroi Jones (Amiri Baraka), and Robert Creeley.

The poetry flyers also document New York City area reading venues of the period. In addition to readings at academic and cultural venues, such as the Crypt in St. Paul's Chapel, Columbia University, the most common venues were in lower Manhattan at St. Mark's Church-in-the-Bowery, which became home to the St. Mark's Poetry Project in 1966, and the Folklore Center at 321 6th Avenue.

did.unittitle: Affidavit with respect to financial affairs
